---
title: "Creating a swap file using a internal secondary hard drive?"
date: 2013-01-12
forum: New to Ubuntu
---

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
I have one STA 64 hard drive and a 32 IDE hard drive 

The STA has my os on it and i would like to create a swap file on the IDE hard drive 

How would I Do this?

Im using 64 bit 12.10

---

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
Welcome to the Ubuntu Forums :-)

Can you see the IDE drive with the following command?

Please post the output of

```
sudo fdisk -lu
```

The next step would be to create a swap partition and then check its UUID and use it in the file [FONT="Courier New"][SIZE="3"]/etc/fstab[/SIZE][/FONT].

Then after reboot, the swap partition should be found and used. Check that with
```
top
``` or ```
swapon -s
```

---

### Post by arpanaut on 2013-01-12
on this page about half way down is instruction for creating a swap file.
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/SwapFaq](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/SwapFaq)

See, Four step process to add swap file, you would need to adjust for your drive layout.

---

### Post by mapes12 on 2013-01-12
How much RAM do you have? Many users don't bother with a Swap file these days. I recently installed Ubu on an oldish EEPPCEE with only about 512MB of RAM without swap and it's running fine.

---

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
> **sudodus said:**
> Welcome to the Ubuntu Forums :-)



thanks XD

Would i put /media/name of drive instead of /mnt/512MiB.swap
when making the swap file?

---

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
You have two swap partitions already

```
/dev/sdb5 118274048 120102911 914432 82 Linux swap / Solaris
/dev/sdb7 116445184 118269951 912384 82 Linux swap / Solaris
```
but they seem somewhat mixed up with encryption.

Do you have encrypted home directories?

What is shown by the following command?
```
swapon -s
```

On which drive do you want to swap: /dev/sda or /dev/sdb ?

---

### Post by mapes12 on 2013-01-12
Those are good specs. I guess a swap file requirement depends on what you will be running. If you have RAM intensive apps e.g. graphics and video editing, then you may need more RAM. Hardware RAM is much faster than a swap file. A swap file these days is somewhat steamage in view of the latent delay that data needs to be saved and then retrieved from the HDD. If you find that your RAM is struggling to cope then think about having Ubu inlitialise zram. It's a way of compressing more RAM out of your hardware. Have a Google read up on it. Current Kernels support its use and it's much faster than a HDD swap file.

---

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
> **64Buntu said:**
> thanks XD

Would i put /media/name of drive instead of /mnt/512MiB.swap
when making the swap file?
Do you want it on the 250 GB drive (/dev/sda), or on the 61.5 GB drive (/dev/sdb)?

---

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
Also decide if you want to hibernate. In that case you need swap to save what is in RAM, to be save at least as much as your RAM.

Otherwise you can have less than the RAM, so that your computer won't freeze when running out of RAM. Instead it gets slow, but you are warned and can close some application and wait for it to do the swapping and then get going again.

---

yes my home folder is encrypted

and I have no idea as to which is which (sda  sbd)

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
Sorry forgot the result of the swapon -s command:

Filename                                                     Type        Size             Used       Priority
/dev/mapper/cryptswap1                  partition    917500    148696    -1

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
> **64Buntu said:**
> Ill will be using eclipse with many plug-ins and running android emulators plus i would like to get every thing out of my computer

yes my home folder is encrypted

and I have no idea as to which is which (sda  sbd)
At least you should know which drive is bigger, and if you want swap on that one or on the other one. Or put it this way: Do you want swap on the same drive as the operating system or on the other one? If you post the output of
```
df
``` we'll see which drive and partition you are running as the root file system.

---

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
I would like to swap on the one that is not my OS 

i would like to swap on 61 gb hard drive

---

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
df:

Filesystem              1K-blocks    Used Available Use% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/ubuntu-root 239209032 5023904 222033936   3% /
udev                       431380       4    431376   1% /dev
tmpfs                      177760     864    176896   1% /run
none                         5120       0      5120   0% /run/lock
none                       444392     160    444232   1% /run/shm
none                       102400      32    102368   1% /run/user
/dev/sda1                  233191   67575    153175  31% /boot

/dev/sdb6                24821880  176048  23384932   1% /media/nic/95589fad-1671-459f-bf62-32ae26b897af

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
I do not care for hibernating as I will either be on my computer or not and i would like to swap the 61.5gb hard drive =sbd

---

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
> **64Buntu said:**
> I do not care for hibernating as I will either be on my computer or not and i would like to swap the 61.5gb hard drive =sbd

From what I see, you are running the system from drive sda, and you have swap on sdb. Like it should be.

Obviously you are using swap, as can be seen from the swapon output. So I suggest that you increase the swap on sdb.

Before starting to edit partitions, ***backup what is really important to save***! There is a small risk, that you will destroy your partition table or file system. But sometimes it happens, and then it is very nice to have the data somewhere else. Only 1 % is used on sdb1 and sdb6, so it's rather small amounts of data to backup.

What do you have on sdb6?
```
/dev/sdb6 24821880 176048 23384932 1% /media/nic/95589fad-1671-459f-bf62-32ae26b897af
```

```
Device Boot Start End Blocks Id System
 /dev/sdb1 * 2048 66005392 33001672+ 83 Linux
 /dev/sdb2 66007038 120102911 27047937 5 Extended
 /dev/sdb5 118274048 120102911 914432 82 Linux swap / Solaris
 /dev/sdb6 66007040 116443135 25218048 83 Linux
 /dev/sdb7 116445184 118269951 912384 82 Linux swap / Solaris
```

You can merge sdb5 and sdb7 and get about 1,8 GB of RAM, because they are next to each other on the disk. That might be enough, since you don't plan to hibernate. If you want more swap, you can shrink sdb6 and use the free space created to merge into the new swap space.

All these operations should be made when booted from another drive. It should be OK from you installed system or from an Ubuntu install drive. Use ***Gparted***!

You need to install it into your installed system:
```
sudo apt-get install gparted
``` Then run it with
```
gksudo gparted
```

---

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
After you have created the new swap partition, you should run
```
sudo blkid
```
to find the UUID of the swap partition. Use the UUID to identfy it in a line to specify (initiate) swap in the file
/etc/fstab. Edit with superuser privileges!

```
sudo nano /etc/fstab
```

---

### Post by Elfy on 2013-01-12
Pretty sure that gparted will see the encrypted swap as unallocated and not recognise it. Unless it's recently changed that is ;)

---

its was a hidden trash file which i have since removed

How would i merge sdb5 and sdb7? 

I reinstalled yesterday so i have nothing important yet

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
All that gparted see's are the sda files how do I have to do anything to see the sdb files's?

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
if you look at this (top left) and the last img the file sizes corrispond yet it says sda and i wanted to make the swap on the sdb

---

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
okay ive found the sdb but its size corresponds with the file size of my main hard drive? that i thought was sda if so i want to make the swap file on the smaller sda?



I have got the uuid of the hard drives i just am not sure about the sdb and sda scenario
 Would I damage my OS by editing the sda?

---

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-12
I have partitioned the sda (had nothing to lose) to a swap file

---

### Post by sudodus on 2013-01-12
> **64Buntu said:**
> You ask what was on the sdb6? 

its was a hidden trash file which i have since removed

How would i merge sdb5 and sdb7? 

I reinstalled yesterday so i have nothing important yet
1. The drive that was /dev/sdb in the output from
```
sudo fdisk -lu
``` in post #5 (61.5 GB) seems to have moved to /dev/sda, when viewed with gparted in post #22.
Anyway, if you have nothing important on that drive, you can use as much as you like for swap, but you can hardly use more than twice the size of the RAM.

If you need a lot of memory, I recommend that you use maximum RAM, that can be plugged into your computer.

Another thing to consider is the speed of the disks. You can test the speed writing some big files to them, and see if the speed will be different. If different, use the fastest disk for swap! I guess it is easiest to compare if you use the same file system and no encryption for such a test. If the speed is similar, it would probably be an advantage to use the 'other' disk, not the one with the operating system for swap. (This is what you intend, so it's OK, except that you should check that this disk is reasonably fast.)

2. The drive that was /dev/sda in the output from
sudo fdisk -lu in post #5 (250 GB) seems to have moved to /dev/sdb, when viewed with gparted in post #22.
--
I don't know why they have switched sda <--> sdb, but you can use the disk size to identify each disk, so that you get it correct when using gparted ... which you are on the way to do according to the picture in post #25.

Now, say that you can increase the RAM to 4 GB and do that. Then I would recommend using 4-8 GB swap, no more than twice the RAM, because you will most likely never use more. So you can use the rest of that drive for a file system, where you can store files. Either an extra storage for files, or to keep a backup copy of important files (documents, pictures ...).

To finally write the changes with gparted, you must click on the [COLOR="SeaGreen"]green tick icon[/COLOR] at the top of the gparted window.

---

### Post by 64Buntu on 2013-01-13
okay thank you I understand now

Thank you to all the helped :)
